rationale: Art.5(1)(a) requires PII processing to have a lawful basis; A.1.2.3 'Identify Lawful Basis' directly implements this by requiring the controller to document a lawful basis for each processing purpose.

Art.5(1)(a) fairness sub-principle requires processing not to adversely affect data subjects in unexpected ways; A.1.3.3 'Information for PII Principals' partially addresses fairness by mandating disclosure of processing details but does not cover all fairness dimensions.

Art.5(1)(a) transparency sub-principle requires open, clear communication about processing; A.1.3.3 covers provision of information to PII principals but is narrower than the full GDPR transparency obligation (which also covers Art.12 modalities).
Art.5(1)(a) transparency also requires accessible delivery of privacy information; A.1.3.4 'Providing Information' covers the delivery mechanism, addressing the provision sub-aspect of transparency.
Art.5(1)(b) restricts use to specified, explicit, legitimate purposes; A.1.2.2 'Identify and Document Purpose' is co-extensive, requiring documented purpose statements for each processing activity.

Art.5(1)(b) further use compatibility test requires assessing whether secondary use is compatible with original purpose; A.1.4.3 'Limit Processing' restricts processing to documented purposes, partially but not fully capturing the compatibility-assessment obligation.
Art.5(1)(c) data minimisation requires PII to be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary; A.1.4.5 'PII Minimisation' is co-extensive with this obligation.
Art.5(1)(c) also applies at collection stage; A.1.4.2 'Limit Collection' addresses the collection-stage aspect, implementing a specific facet of Art.5(1)(c).
Art.5(1)(d) requires PII to be accurate and kept up to date, with inaccurate data erased or rectified; A.1.4.4 'Accuracy and Quality' is directly co-extensive with this obligation.

Art.5(1)(e) prohibits retaining PII in identifiable form longer than necessary; A.1.4.8 'Retention' requires defining and enforcing retention periods, which is the operational implementation of this principle.
Art.5(1)(e) also requires secure deletion at end of retention; A.1.4.9 'Disposal' covers the end-of-life deletion obligation as a specific sub-aspect.
Art.5(1)(f) integrity and confidentiality principle requires appropriate security including encryption; A.3.26 'Use of Cryptography' implements the encryption sub-aspect of this security principle.
Art.5(1)(f) also requires appropriate access controls to ensure confidentiality; A.3.9 'Access Rights' addresses the access-control dimension of this principle.
Art.5(1)(f) integrity dimension requires detection of unauthorised access; A.3.25 'Logging' implements the detection/monitoring sub-aspect by requiring audit logs of PII access.
Art.5(1)(f) requires availability and resilience of processing systems; A.3.24 'Information Backup' addresses the availability and recovery sub-aspect.

Art.5(2) accountability principle requires the controller to be able to demonstrate compliance; A.1.2.9 'Records of Processing PII' provides the primary evidential mechanism for controller accountability.
Art.5(2) accountability also requires demonstrating ongoing compliance through review; A.3.15 'Independent Review of Information Security' addresses the audit/review sub-aspect of accountability.
Art.6(1)(a) establishes consent as a lawful basis; A.1.2.3 'Identify Lawful Basis' covers consent as one of six bases, making this a subset relationship (A.1.2.3 is broader than one specific basis).

Art.6(1)(a) requires valid consent to have been obtained; A.1.2.4 'Determine Consent' is co-extensive, requiring assessment of whether and when consent is the appropriate basis and what conditions must be met.

Art.6(1)(b-f) enumerate five additional lawful bases (contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interests); A.1.2.3 requires identifying one of these bases but does not elaborate on their substantive requirements, so the GDPR is broader here.
Art.7(1) requires the controller to demonstrate that consent was given; A.1.2.5 'Obtain and Record Consent' is co-extensive, requiring records of consent acquisition and conditions.

Art.7(2) requires consent requests to be clearly distinguishable and presented in an intelligible form; A.1.2.4 'Determine Consent' addresses conditions for valid consent but does not prescribe presentation format, making this a partial overlap.
Art.7(3) grants data subjects the right to withdraw consent at any time; A.1.3.5 'Modify or Withdraw Consent' is directly co-extensive with this obligation.

Art.7(4) prohibits conditioning service on unnecessary consent; A.2.2.4 'Marketing and Advertising Use' restricts processors from using PII for marketing without consent, addressing one specific conditioned-consent scenario.

Art.12(1) requires information to be provided in concise, transparent, intelligible form; A.1.3.3 'Information for PII Principals' is co-extensive, requiring appropriate privacy notices to be provided.

Art.12(2) requires the controller to facilitate the exercise of data subject rights; A.1.3.10 'Handling Requests' is co-extensive, requiring a process for receiving and responding to PII principal requests.

Art.12(3) specifies that responses must be provided without undue delay and within one month; A.1.3.10 covers request handling but the one-month specific timeframe is a GDPR-specific requirement not fully captured in the control.

Art.12(4) governs refusal of requests including information to provide and rights to complain; A.1.3.10 'Handling Requests' covers the handling process but does not specifically address refusal justification procedures.

Art.13(1) requires specific information to be provided at time of collection from the data subject; A.1.3.3 'Information for PII Principals' and A.1.3.4 'Providing Information' together implement this requirement.
Art.13(1) delivery mechanism (at collection time, directly to data subject); A.1.3.4 'Providing Information' implements the delivery modality aspect of this obligation.

Art.13(2) lists additional information to provide (retention periods, right to withdraw, right to complain etc.); A.1.3.3 covers the general information obligation but the specific Art.13(2) list items represent granular obligations not enumerated in the control.
Art.14 extends the Art.13 information obligation to indirect collection scenarios; A.1.3.3 applies to all PII collection regardless of source, covering both direct and indirect collection.

Art.14(3) specifies that for indirectly collected data, information must be provided within one month or at first contact; A.1.3.4 covers provision of information but does not specify Art.14's specific timeframe.

Art.15(1) grants data subjects the right to obtain confirmation of processing and access to their PII; A.1.3.7 'Access, Correction or Erasure' is co-extensive, covering the access dimension of data subject rights.
Art.15(3) specifically grants the right to receive a copy of personal data undergoing processing; A.1.3.9 'Providing Copy of PII' is directly and co-extensively targeted at this sub-requirement.
Art.15(4) limits the copy right where it adversely affects the rights of others; A.1.3.9 addresses providing copies but does not directly prescribe the third-party rights balancing test.

Art.16 grants the right to rectification of inaccurate or incomplete personal data; A.1.3.7 'Access, Correction or Erasure' covers rectification (correction) as one of three bundled rights addressed by this single control.
Art.17(1) grants the right to erasure under specified conditions (purpose lapsed, consent withdrawn, etc.); A.1.3.7 covers erasure as part of its bundled access/correction/erasure control.
Art.17(2) requires the controller to inform third parties who received the PII of any erasure request; A.1.3.8 'Inform Third Parties' is directly co-extensive with this sub-obligation.

Art.18 grants the right to restrict processing under specific conditions; A.1.3.7 'Access, Correction or Erasure' covers restriction as part of the bundled rights mechanism, though restriction is not separately enumerated.

Art.19 requires the controller to notify each recipient to whom PII was disclosed of any rectification, erasure or restriction; A.1.3.8 'Inform Third Parties' is directly and co-extensively targeted at this notification obligation.

Art.20(1) grants the right to receive PII in structured, commonly used, machine-readable format; A.1.3.9 'Providing Copy of PII' covers this portability-as-copy obligation, though the machine-readable format requirement is a GDPR-specific detail beyond the control's text.

Art.20(2) further grants the right to transmit PII directly to another controller; A.1.3.9 addresses providing copies to the data subject but direct controller-to-controller transmission is not fully covered.
Art.21(1) grants the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests or public task; A.1.3.6 'Object to PII Processing' is co-extensive with this right.

Art.21(2-3) provides an absolute right to object to direct marketing processing; A.1.3.6 covers objection rights generally, but does not specifically call out the absolute nature of the direct-marketing objection right.

Art.22(1) grants the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects; A.1.3.11 'Automated Decision Making' is directly co-extensive with this obligation.

Art.22(2) enumerates exceptions to the automated-decision prohibition (consent, contract, law) with safeguards; A.1.3.11 covers the overarching automated decision obligation but the specific exception conditions are GDPR-specific details not enumerated in the control.

Art.22(3) mandates human intervention, right to express view, and right to contest automated decisions in exception cases; A.1.3.11 addresses the overall control but does not specify these procedural safeguards.

Art.25(1) requires data protection to be implemented from the design phase, embedding data minimisation by default; A.1.4.2 'Limit Collection' operationalises privacy-by-design at the collection stage.

Art.25(1) requires data protection considerations to be integrated into system design; A.3.27 'Secure Development Life Cycle' implements this by requiring privacy-relevant security controls to be built into development processes.
Art.25(1) privacy-by-design mandate includes architecture-level decisions; A.3.29 'Secure System Architecture and Engineering Principles' implements privacy-by-design at the architectural level.

Art.25(2) requires that by default only PII necessary for each specific purpose is processed; A.1.4.3 'Limit Processing' is co-extensive, requiring processing to be restricted to documented purposes with minimised scope.
Art.25(2) also requires default settings to minimise data without user intervention; A.1.4.5 'PII Minimisation' directly implements the data minimisation aspect of privacy-by-default.

Art.26(1) requires joint controllers to determine their respective responsibilities in a transparent arrangement; A.1.2.8 'Joint PII Controller' is directly co-extensive, requiring documented joint controller arrangements.

Art.26(2) requires the essence of the joint controller arrangement to be made available to data subjects; A.1.2.8 covers joint controller documentation but does not specifically require making the essence publicly available.

Art.26(3) allows data subjects to exercise their rights against any joint controller regardless of the internal arrangement; A.1.2.8 addresses the arrangement structure but not the single-point-of-contact obligation.

Art.28(1) requires controllers to use only processors providing sufficient guarantees; A.1.2.7 'Contracts with PII Processors' implements this by requiring appropriate contractual and evidential safeguards for processor selection.

Art.28(2) requires processor to obtain controller authorisation before engaging sub-processors; A.2.5.7 'Disclosure of Subcontractors' and A.2.5.8 together cover the sub-processor consent and disclosure obligations.

Art.28(2) sub-processor engagement requires controller prior authorisation; A.2.5.8 'Engagement of a Subcontractor to Process PII' is co-extensive with the requirement to obtain prior written authorisation before sub-processor engagement.

Art.28(2) also requires the processor to notify controllers of intended changes concerning sub-processors; A.2.5.9 'Change of Subcontractor to Process PII' is directly co-extensive with this change-notification obligation.

Art.28(3)(a) requires the processor to process PII only on documented instructions from the controller; A.2.2.2 'Customer Agreement' is co-extensive, requiring a contractual basis for all processing that specifies scope and instructions.

Art.28(3)(a) prohibits the processor from using PII for its own purposes; A.2.2.3 'Organisation's Purposes' directly implements this by requiring processors to process PII only for the customer's documented purposes.
Art.28(3)(b) requires all persons authorised to process PII to have committed to confidentiality; A.3.18 'Confidentiality or Non-Disclosure Agreements' is co-extensive with this obligation.
Art.28(3)(c) requires the processor to implement Art.32 security measures; A.3.26 'Use of Cryptography' addresses the encryption dimension of required security measures.

Art.28(3)(e) requires the processor to assist the controller in fulfilling data subject rights obligations; A.2.3.2 'Comply with Obligations to PII Principals' is co-extensive with this assistance obligation.

Art.28(3)(f-g) requires the processor to delete or return all PII at the end of service; A.2.4.3 'Return, Transfer or Disposal of PII' is directly co-extensive with this end-of-contract data handling obligation.

Art.28(3)(h) requires the processor to allow and contribute to audits; A.3.15 'Independent Review of Information Security' covers the audit/review obligation but from the organisation's own perspective rather than specifically as an obligation owed to the controller.

Art.28(4) requires sub-processors to be bound by the same data protection obligations as the processor; A.2.5.8 covers engagement of sub-processors but does not explicitly require flow-down of identical obligations.
Art.30(1) requires controllers to maintain records of processing activities (RoPA); A.1.2.9 'Records of Processing PII' is co-extensive with this record-keeping obligation for controllers.

Art.30(2) requires processors to maintain records of all categories of processing on behalf of controllers; A.2.2.7 'Records Related to Processing PII' is co-extensive with this processor-side record-keeping obligation.
Art.30(3) requires records to be in writing, including electronic form; A.1.2.9 covers records of processing but does not prescribe the electronic/written format specifically.

Art.30(4) requires records to be made available to the supervisory authority on request; A.1.2.9 ensures records are maintained but does not specifically address supervisory authority access obligations.
Art.32(1)(a) requires pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data as a technical measure; A.3.26 'Use of Cryptography' is co-extensive with the encryption sub-requirement.
Art.32(1)(a) also requires pseudonymisation; A.1.4.6 'De-identification and Deletion' covers de-identification/pseudonymisation as a privacy technique, addressing this specific sub-aspect.
Art.32(1)(b) requires ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems; A.3.9 'Access Rights' implements the confidentiality dimension through access control.
Art.32(1)(b) availability requirement includes backup and recovery capabilities; A.3.24 'Information Backup' implements the availability/resilience sub-aspect.

Art.32(1)(c) specifically requires ability to restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner following a physical or technical incident; A.3.24 'Information Backup' is co-extensive with this restore-capability requirement.

Art.32(1)(d) requires a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating security measures; A.3.15 'Independent Review of Information Security' is co-extensive with this testing and evaluation obligation.

Art.32(2) requires taking into account risks to individuals' rights when implementing security; A.3.3 'Information Security Policies' sets the policy framework but the specific risk-based calibration of Art.32(2) requires more than a policy framework alone.

Art.32(4) requires the processor to act only on controller instructions for security measures; A.2.2.2 'Customer Agreement' covers the contractual instruction requirement that underpins this obligation.

Art.33(1) requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach; A.3.11 'Incident Management Planning and Preparation' is co-extensive with establishing the processes needed to meet this timeframe.

Art.33(2) requires processors to notify the controller without undue delay after discovering a breach; A.3.12 'Response to Information Security Incidents' implements the response and notification obligations including processor-to-controller escalation.

Art.33(3) specifies the mandatory content of breach notifications (nature, categories, approximate numbers, etc.); A.3.11 covers incident management planning but does not enumerate specific notification content requirements.

Art.33(5) requires documentation of all personal data breaches including facts, effects and remedial actions; A.3.14 'Protection of Records' is co-extensive with this documentation and records retention obligation for breach records.

Art.34(1) requires communicating high-risk breaches to affected data subjects without undue delay; A.3.12 'Response to Information Security Incidents' covers the incident response process including communication to affected parties.

Art.34(2) specifies content requirements for communications to data subjects (clear description, DPO contact, likely consequences, measures taken); A.3.12 covers incident response but does not enumerate the specific communication content elements.

Art.34(3) provides exceptions to the communication obligation (encryption applied, measures eliminating risk, disproportionate effort); A.3.12 does not address these exception conditions specifically.
Art.35(1) requires a DPIA for processing likely to result in high risk to individuals; A.1.2.6 'Privacy Impact Assessment' is co-extensive with this DPIA requirement.

Art.35(3) lists specific processing types that always require a DPIA (systematic profiling, large-scale special categories, systematic public monitoring); A.1.2.6 covers the general PIA requirement but does not enumerate these mandatory triggers.

Art.35(7) specifies mandatory DPIA content (description, necessity assessment, risk assessment, mitigating measures); A.1.2.6 covers conducting a PIA but does not prescribe the specific content elements required.
Art.35(8-9) requires the DPO to be consulted in the DPIA process and results to be taken into account; A.1.2.6 covers the PIA process but does not specify DPO consultation as a mandatory component.

Art.37(1) requires designation of a DPO in specified circumstances; A.3.4 'Information Security Roles and Responsibilities' covers privacy-related role assignment but the specific DPO designation obligation is a GDPR-specific legal requirement not fully captured in a general roles control.

Art.37(5) requires the DPO to be designated on the basis of professional qualities and expert knowledge of data protection law; A.3.4 addresses role-based responsibilities but does not specify the qualification requirements for the DPO role.

Art.38 requires the DPO to be involved in all data protection matters and be supported with resources; A.3.4 addresses role definition but organisational support, independence, and involvement requirements go beyond what the control prescribes.

Art.44 prohibits transfers to third countries without an adequate transfer basis; A.1.5.2 'Basis for PII Transfer' is co-extensive with this requirement to document and validate the legal basis for each international transfer.
Art.44 applies equally to processor-side transfers; A.2.5.2 'Basis for PII Transfer Between Jurisdictions' is co-extensive from the processor perspective.

Art.46(1) enumerates appropriate safeguards for transfers (SCCs, BCRs, approved codes of conduct, certification etc.); A.1.5.2 requires a documented basis for transfer but does not enumerate the specific Art.46 safeguard mechanisms.

Art.46 requires identification of countries/organisations to which transfers are permitted; A.1.5.3 'Countries for PII Transfer' is co-extensive with this requirement to document destination jurisdictions.
Art.46 applies to processor-side international transfers; A.2.5.3 'Countries and International Organizations for PII Transfer' is co-extensive from the processor perspective.

Art.5(1)(f) security principle encompasses appropriate classification of information to protect it; A.3.5 'Classification of Information' implements the classification sub-aspect of the integrity and confidentiality principle.
Art.5(1)(f) requires preventing unauthorised access to personal data through identity controls; A.3.8 'Identity Management' implements the identity-verification dimension of the security principle.
Art.5(1)(f) requires appropriate authentication mechanisms to restrict access to personal data; A.3.23 'Secure Authentication' implements the authentication sub-aspect of the security principle.

Art.32(1)(b) requires security of processing systems including endpoint controls; A.3.22 'User Endpoint Devices' implements endpoint security as a sub-component of the overall security-of-processing requirement.

Art.32(1)(b) requires technical measures for confidentiality including strong authentication; A.3.23 'Secure Authentication' implements the authentication dimension of the required technical measures.

Art.30(1)(e) specifically requires records of third-country transfers including documentation of safeguards; A.1.5.4 'Records of PII Transfer' implements this specific transfer-records sub-requirement of Art.30.

Art.30(2)(c) requires processor records to include details of transfers to third countries; A.2.5.4 'Records of PII Disclosures to Third Parties' covers the disclosure/transfer records aspect of Art.30(2).

Art.28(3)(d) requires the processor to assist the controller with DPIA and Art.36 prior consultation; A.1.2.6 'Privacy Impact Assessment' covers the controller-side DPIA but not explicitly the processor assistance obligation.

Art.28(3)(d) processor obligation to assist with DPIA/security/breach notification; A.2.3.2 'Comply with Obligations to PII Principals' covers the general processor assistance obligation but the DPIA-specific assistance is not explicitly scoped.

Art.5(2) accountability requires demonstrating compliance with policies and standards; A.3.16 'Compliance with Policies, Rules and Standards' implements the compliance-demonstration sub-aspect of accountability.
Art.32(1)(b) security measures extend to physical media containing personal data; A.3.20 'Storage Media' implements the physical media security sub-aspect of processing security.
Art.32(1)(b) security of processing requires secure disposal of equipment that processed PII; A.3.21 'Secure Disposal or Re-use of Equipment' implements the equipment disposal sub-aspect.

Art.32(1)(b) technical security measures include application-level security controls; A.3.28 'Application Security Requirements' implements the application security sub-aspect of the processing security obligation.
Art.25(1) privacy-by-design requires security requirements to be embedded in applications; A.3.28 'Application Security Requirements' implements this at the application specification level.
Art.25(1) requires privacy to be considered during design/testing phases; A.3.31 'Test Information' restricts use of real PII in testing, implementing one specific privacy-by-design obligation.

Art.5(1)(a) transparency also requires disclosure of third-party disclosures to data subjects; A.2.5.5 'PII Disclosure Requests' covers processor handling of such disclosure requests from relevant authorities.

Art.6(4) requires a compatibility assessment before using PII for a different purpose; A.1.2.2 'Identify and Document Purpose' covers purpose documentation but does not explicitly operationalise the compatibility test methodology.

Art.13(2)(e) requires informing data subjects of their right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; A.1.3.3 covers information provision generally but the right-to-complain specific element is a GDPR-specific disclosure item.

Art.17(3) enumerates overriding grounds that can prevent erasure (legal obligation, public interest, archiving, legal claims); A.1.3.7 covers the erasure mechanism but not the balancing test for these exceptions.

Art.21(3) absolute right to object to direct marketing processing applies equally to processors facilitating such processing; A.2.2.4 'Marketing and Advertising Use' restricts processors from processing PII for marketing purposes without explicit permission, implementing this absolute restriction.
Art.30(4) requires processor records to be available to supervisory authorities; A.2.2.7 covers processor record maintenance but not specifically the supervisory authority access obligation.

Art.33(4) allows phased notifications to the SA where complete information is not available within 72 hours; A.3.11 incident management planning should accommodate phased notification but this specific GDPR provision is not explicitly captured.

Art.37(2) permits a group of undertakings to designate a single DPO accessible from each establishment; A.3.4 addresses role assignment but the group/multi-entity DPO model is a GDPR-specific structural option not addressed in the control.

Art.44 prohibition requires comprehensive records of all disclosures as evidence of compliance; A.1.5.5 'Records of PII Disclosures' supports the evidential basis for demonstrating lawful transfer compliance.

Art.46(2)(c) specifically authorises Standard Contractual Clauses as a transfer mechanism; A.1.5.2 requires a documented transfer basis without specifying which Art.46 mechanism to use, so GDPR is more specific here.

Art.28(3)(c) requires processor to implement security measures per Art.32 including architectural safeguards; A.3.29 'Secure System Architecture and Engineering Principles' implements the architecture dimension of required security measures.

Art.5(1)(b) purpose limitation applies to processors who must not use PII beyond controller instructions; A.2.2.3 'Organisation's Purposes' is co-extensive with the processor obligation not to exceed the specified processing purposes.

Art.28(3)(f) end-of-service deletion requirement relates to de-identification as a privacy-protective technique; A.1.4.6 'De-identification and Deletion' covers both techniques but is framed as a controller-side data minimisation measure rather than a processor end-of-contract obligation.

Art.35(6) requires prior consultation with the SA when DPIA reveals residual high risk; A.1.2.6 'Privacy Impact Assessment' covers the PIA process but does not specifically trigger or describe the prior consultation pathway.
Art.5(1)(f) confidentiality principle extends to data in transit; A.3.7 'Information Transfer' implements the in-transit security sub-aspect of the integrity and confidentiality principle.
Art.32(1)(b) physical security sub-aspects include prevention of unauthorised viewing of PII on screens and desks; A.3.19 'Clear Desk and Clear Screen' implements this physical security sub-aspect.

Art.28(1) requires assessing processor sufficiency which includes reviewing supplier security controls; A.3.10 'Addressing Information Security within Supplier Agreements' implements the supplier-security-assessment sub-aspect of processor due diligence.

Art.5(2) accountability requires staff awareness and training to demonstrate compliance; A.3.17 'Information Security Awareness, Education and Training' implements the staff training sub-aspect of the accountability principle.

Art.32(1)(b) technical measures must include logging to detect and respond to confidentiality breaches; A.3.25 'Logging' implements the detection-through-audit-logging sub-aspect of processing security.

GDPR implicitly requires processors not to execute instructions that would infringe the regulation; A.2.2.5 'Infringing Instruction' explicitly addresses the obligation to refuse or flag instructions that would cause non-compliance.

GDPR Art.28 creates controller-to-processor obligations; A.2.2.6 'Customer Obligations' covers the processor's obligation to ensure the controller meets their own GDPR obligations regarding the processing.

Art.28(3)(e) requires processors to assist with breach notification (Art.33/34); A.2.5.5 'Notification of PII Disclosure Requests' covers processor notification obligations when PII is requested or disclosed to third parties.

Art.28(3)(e) assistance obligation includes handling legally binding disclosure demands; A.2.5.6 'Legally Binding PII Disclosures' addresses the specific sub-case of compelled disclosures under legal mandate.

GDPR Art.4(11) defines consent as freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous indication of agreement; A.1.2.4 'Determine Consent' must operationalise this definition but the four-element GDPR definition is broader than what the control explicitly requires.

Art.9(2)(a) requires explicit consent for special category data processing; A.1.2.4 'Determine Consent' covers consent conditions generally but the heightened explicit-consent standard for special categories is a GDPR-specific requirement not explicitly differentiated in the control.

Art.5(1)(a) transparency principle requires compliance with applicable legal requirements; A.3.13 'Legal, Statutory, Regulatory and Contractual Requirements' covers the broader obligation to identify and comply with legal obligations including transparency requirements.
